query: A nutritionist collected daily calorie intake (kcal) from 10 participants: [1850,1920,2010,1780,1960,2050,1890,1830,1975,2020]. She wants to test whether mean intake differs significantly from the recommended 2000 kcal/day — but only if the data passes a normality check. What is her conclusion?

A data analyst suspects outliers are masking the true relationship between two variables. Dataset A: [1,2,3,4,5,50], Dataset B: [2,4,6,8,10,12]. First find any outliers in A, then compute the correlation on the cleaned dataset and compare with the raw correlation.

Website load times (ms) over 10 requests: [210,215,220,218,212,680,214,219,211,217]. Provide a full picture: summary stats on raw data, flag anomalies, then give a 95% CI for the true mean load time using only clean data.

Temperature readings (°C) were [15,18,22,25,30] and ice cream sales were [200,250,320,380,450]. Standardise both variables and confirm their correlation, then report the regression slope on the original (un-standardised) data.

A hedge fund tracks daily returns (%): [-0.5,0.3,0.8,-0.2,1.2,0.5,-0.1,0.9,0.4,5.8,-4.2,0.6]. Run a full distributional audit: quartiles, IQR, outlier detection via IQR method, then a 95% CI on the cleaned series.

A clinical trial recorded post-treatment pain scores [3,4,3,5,4,3,4,6,3,4] (scale 1-10). The treatment claims to reduce pain below the population mean of 5. Verify normality, summarise the data, then test whether the mean is significantly below 5.

Weekly revenue ($k) for 12 weeks: [42,45,43,47,50,48,52,55,53,57,60,58]. First smooth the series with a 3-week moving average, then fit a linear regression to the smoothed values to estimate the weekly growth trend.
